Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance

Dear Mr. Nelson:
This is in response to your letter requesting further advice
as general counsel. to the contra costa water District regarding
the respons ibil ities of tto consultants to the district pursuant
to the Lonfl ict-of-interest provisions of the PoIitical Reforn Act
(the I'Actrr1.1 You have asked for clarification of sorne of the
points and conclusions reached in our letter to willian J' woska,
issistant General uanager of the contra costa water District (our
File No. I-91-286). Since your request seeks further guidance
with respect to tire analysil in the EegkE letter, we are treating
your ."qire.t as^one for infornal assistance pursuant to Regulation
18329 (b) (8) (c) ..
OUESTTONS
1. can a corporation be a rrconsultanttr under the Act?
2. If a consultant does not make a final decision, is the
consultant stilI subject to the disclosure and disqua I i f ication
provisions of the Act?
1 Gor".rrr"nt code sections 81ooo-91015. A11 statutory references
are to the covernnent code unless otherwise indicated. Cornrnission
regulations appear at 2 california Code of Regulations Section
18ooo, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California code of Regulations.
2 rnfornal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
imrnunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section
83114 i Regulation 18329 (c) (3) . )
428 J Street, Suite 8OO . P.O. Box 807 o Sacramento CA 958044807 o (9 16) 3 22.5660

3. To what extent does the express language of a contract
betlreen a firD and the district alter the conclusion that the
persons providing services to the district are consultants?
4. What constitutes contract monitoring, in contrast to
control and direction of the district?
5. Does the definition of rrparticipates in the rnaking of a
governmental decisionrr conflict with the exception in Regulation
18700 (a) (2) ?
CONCLUSIONS
1. A rrconsultantn as defined in Regulation 18700(a) (2) nust
be a natural person and cannot be a business entity.
2. A rrconsultantrr is a Person who provides under contract,
inforuatlon, advlce, reconDendatlon or counsel to a state or local
government agency. The definitlon does not require that the
f.r"on rp3;1gri a itnal declsion. lttoreover, the exception in
hegulation 18700 applles only if both of the.follolrinql aPply:
(1i the consultant conducts research and arrives at conclusions
iiLn ..rp."t to his or her rendltion of lnformation, advice,
iectnnen'aatlon, or counsel independent of control and dlrectlon of
ih. "g.n"y; ana, (2) the consullant possesses no authority with
="spe6t to'any agency decision beyond the rendition of
inf6rnation, idvicer- reconDendatlon or counsel. Thus, even where
a consultani does not rrnakerr an agency decislon, the consultant
may not be within the exceptlon lf he or she functions under the
control and direction of the agency.
3. Uodlfication of the language in a consulting contract nay
be useful in setting up a relationstrip which does not result in
the contractor belng considered a trconsultant.rr However, the
factual circurostances will detennine whether the contractor is
acting as a consultant under the Act.
4. where a firrn partlcipates in neetings and discussion with
the agency, as well as third parties on behalf of the agency, the
consultant is perfonoing the terns of the contract subject to the
control and direction oi the publlc agency and is acting rnuch like
an euployee of the agency. Conversely, where agency
reprelentatives are invotved in the firrn's work only to the extent
necessary to ensure that the firrn satisfies the provislons of the
contract, or where a consultant requests factual infornation from
the public altency to coDply r.rith the terms of a contract, such
inteiaction would not ne-essarlly nake the employees of the firm a
rrconsultantsrr within the roeaning of Regnrlation 18700(a) (2).

Page 3
5. Regulation 18700(a) (2) defines when an individual is a
Itconsultant.tr Regulation 18700(c) defines when a consultant
trparticipates in the rnakinq of a governrnental decision. t' If there
is "signiticant interveningJ substantive revielr" of a consultant's
recomrnendations by the governmental agency, the consultant would
not be trparticipating in a governtnental decision.'r
FACTS
You have not provided any additional or modified facts for
consideration in this letter. 
Therefore, lre set forth the facts
of the woska tetter as the basis for this discussion. The contra
costa wiEer District (the 'rdistrictrt) retains tr'ro engineering
firms, Hasseltine-Best and litccill-Uartin-Self , to assist the
district on various water projects.
Ha sse 1t ine-Best
The Assistant General }lanager for the Los vaqueros Project
stated that the firrn of Hasseltine-Best was retained to
partlcipate in the review of work product and in the devel'opnent
irf action plans to inplenent Los vaqueros pipeline alignnent
deslgnations and propLrty acqulsitlon. In that activlty, they
adviie the distrllt 
LoncLrning agencles affected, the relationship
of particular alignrnents to proposed developnent projects or
proierty-owner inierests. they nay reconnend courses of action,
contacts and speclflc ProPosals.
The Assistant General Hanager for Englneerlng descrlbes
Hasseltine-Best's job duties as related to general engineering
assignnents including attending neetings with district personnel'
and lersons or entities outside of the district to obtain and
sharl infornation. The consultant's role is that of obtalning and
presenting inforroation, as rrell as offering advice as to the
infornation the district night assernble in order to best
conmunicate its work. The consultant forwards and explains
inforrnation prepared by the district. 
The infornation transnitted
is the subJe-t of discussion betrreen the consultant and the
district, Lo ensure clarity and proper interpretatlon by the
consultant.
According to the consulting agreeDent, Hasseltine-Best has a
broad scope oi perforroance. Hassettine-Best will perform services
retated to the dlstrict's Los Vaqueros Project, including
representation of the district at ueetings of public entities and
private organizations and neeting and conferrinq with district
staff and ionsultants as necesEary. The services provided under
the Hasseltine-Best/distrlct contract wiLl be in accordance with
the dlrections, under the control, and to the satisfaction of the
general nanager.

ltcci 11-ltartin-seIf
lfccil l-lrartin-Self was retained to develop technical
inf ornatl,on, maPs, and other materials. Representatives of-the
firrn attend rneelings and coronunicate with district personnel and
others to obtain oi exchange infornation necessary for their work.
The consultant's role is that of obtaining and presenting

The consultint forwards and explains information prepared by the

Representatives of the firro participate in the_review of lrork

'aavice concerniirg proposed courses of action and specific

uith the Clty of Antloch and Contra Costa concerning

According to the consulting agreenent, McGi 1l-lrartin-Self is
to roeet wttfr lhe dlstrlct to provlde assumptions and Parameters
for land use studies and future water service areas and
iu.iifti"".

,iii p..p..e three lanh use inaps based on the data, develop.a plan
for firtuie treated water serviles areas and Lrork with the dlstrict
io-a"""iop a llst of and potentlal sites for future facilities,
corridorsl and canaLs for servlclng future needs and denands'
!,rcai 1l-ila;tln-SeIf wilI also neet with district staff to discuss

agreement states that Phase If will require a great deal. of

that proper direction and progress are rnade.
ANALYSI S
1. Natural Persons
A itconsultantrr is defined in Regulation 18700(a) (2) as
foI lows :
trConsultantrr shall include any natural person
who provides under contract, inforrnation, advice,
reconmendation or counsel to a state or l,ocal
government agency,
Regulation 18700 (a) (2).
According to Regiulation 18700(a) (2), a rrconsultantrr nust be a
natural person and cannot be a business entity.


Our Fi■e No. I-9■-437
Page 5
The rationale behind liuriting the tern consultant to natural
persons is that the actual decisionnaker, as in the.case of a
lublic officia)., wilL be an individual. Moreover disclosure of
the interests of the fir!0 itself does not appear to reflect the
policy goals of the Act. The Act is intended to ensure that a
iuUlil 5fti"i.t 
wiII not be biased in performing his or her dutj.es
Ly the official's financial interests. (Section 81001(b).) The
disclosure of the interests of the firm, in the place of the
interests of the decisionnaker, in Dany cases will' not inforn the
public of such potential sources of bias.
For example, an emPloyee of a firn uray perforrn consulting
services for I public agency concerning a project that may affect
a client of the firrn. is an eurployee, the involvement of the
client nay have no significance to the. enployee-. On-the other
f'ri"a, it fhe ernptoyee ouns property which rrill be affected by his
oi 116. consultiirg iecisions, the enployee nay be biased in the
recornmendatlons he nakes to the publlc entity.
2. Decisionnakinq Authoritv
You have also asked if a person can be a consultant lf the
person does not trDake[ a final decision. Regutation 18700(a) (2)
provides that a ttconsultantrr is a person who provides under
iontract, information. advice, recornmendatio! 9r cqunsel to a
state or I@. 
ttre definition does not require
ifr.t tfr" person rrnake,t a flnal declsion. The conflict of interest
potentlal- ln a consulting arrangenent arises if the person may
ir.31srr or rinfluencerr governnental decisions to further the
person's own financial interests.
Thus, for exanple, where an offj.cial's recornrnendation to a
decisionuraker rnay bL biased, that person has a conflict of
interest. 
(Reguiation 18700.1.) ln addition, where the official
nakes the adcilion and has a financial stake in the decision, the
official has a conflict of interest. (Regulation 1870o.)
Regrulatlon 187oo does provide an exception to the definitlon
of 'rconiultantrr which reqJuires that the consultant conduct
research and arrlve at conclusions lrlth respect to his or her
renditlon of inforrnation, advice, reconnendation, or counsel
independent of control and direction of the agency, and that the
consultant possess no authority with respect to any agency
decision beyond the rendition of infonoation, advice,
reconmendation or counsel.
3. contract Landuaqe
You have also asked whether the conclusion concerninq
Hasseltine-Best would be different if the words rrunder the
controlrr were deleted froro the consulting agreeurent. According to


Our Fl■e NO. I-91-437
Page 6
the consulting agreement, Hasseltine-Best wilI perform services
related to the district's Los vagueros Project in accordance wj.th
the directions, under the control, and to the satisfaction of the
general Danager, This langntage suggests that Hasseltine-Best
fails the first part of the test for the exception. No
infornation was provided with your request to alter this
concl.usion.
Please note, holrever, that rnodification of the contract's
language to reftect the factors in the exception in Regulation
18700(a) (2) }rould not alter the conclusion. clearly the exception
in Regnrlation 18700(a) (2) is a fact-based exception, and not one
which can be net by a nere recital in a contract. For an employee
to be exeropted he or she nust conduct necessary research and
arrive at conclusions !J!ggE!!de.It of control and direction of the
agency or any agency offlcial ggg! possess no authority with
respect to any agency decision beyond the rendition of
infornatlon, advice, reconnendation or counsel. Thus, a
contractual recltal night aid the parties in setting up a
relationship whlch does not result ln the ernployee bei.ng
consldered a [consultantrrI however, the factual circurustances
uould detemine whether the contractor is deened a consultant
under the Act.
You also asked whether Hasseltine-Best would qualify as a
consultant if the firrn nerely relayed infornation fron the
dlstrlct to other parties and then relayed infornatlon obtained
fron the parties back to the dlstrlct. 
In the xaplan Advice
Letter (No. A-82-108), we advised that under sorne circumstances
servlces perforned for a pubtlc agency may be I'rninlsteria lrr in
nature .
[A]n action is roinlsterial, even if it
requires considerable expertise and professional
skiIl, if there is no discretion as to the outcone
(or at least, no discretion with respect to any
part of the result trhich could influence the
governnental decision in question).
***
Holrever, this exceptlon would not apply to
technical tasks, such as nost data gathering or
analysis, in which the consultant nakes
professlonal judgments whlch can affect the
ultirnate decision in question.
Houever, hrhether a decision was indeed ninisterial goes to
hrhether the lndividuals need be deslgnated rather than lrhether
they are public officials under the Act. An individual that


Our File No. I-9■-437
Page 7
provides ninisterial services is not participating in a
governnental decision. (Reg"ulation 18700(c).) nepetition of
rninisterial services would not affect this conclusion.
4. Contract Monitorinq
What constitutes Eere trcontract monitoringtt is not defined in
the Act. Generally, lrhere a firro participates in meetj,ngs and
discussion with the agency, as well as third parties on behalf of
the agency, the consultant is perforning the terns of the contract
subject to the control and dj.rection of the public agency and is
acting nuch tike an ernployee of the agency who is under the
supervision of the public agency staff. 
(See, Criss Advice
Letter, No. A-82-029. ) I{e believe this would be roore than
contract roonitoring.
conversely, rrhere agency representatives are involved in the
firn,s work only to the extent necessary to ensure that the firn
satisfies the provLsions of the contract, or rrhere a consultant
only reguests factual lnfornatlon fron the pub1lc agency to conply
with the terns of a contract, such interaction would not
necessarily Dake the flrn a rconsultantrr within the rneaning of
Regulatlon 18700 (a) (2).
While the point at which interaction becoroes nore than
contract nonitoring is unclear ln the abstract, according to the
facts in the woska Advice Letter, the district has greater
involvement with the finos than nere contract rnonitoring.
According to the consultlng agreeroent vith Hasse ltine-Best,
Hasseltine-Best will perfono services related to the district's
Los Vaqueros Project, including representation of the district at
meetings of public entities and private organizations, and neeting
and conferring with district staff and consultants as necessary.
The services provided under the Hasseltine-Best/district contract
wilt be in accordance trith the dlrections, under the control, and
to the satisfaction of the genera] Eanager. The contract does not
set out clearly defined tasks which the district rnonitors, but
suggests that Hasseltine-Best acts alnost as additional district
staff with respect to the project. Thus, we concluded, based on
the terms of the contract, that the enployees of Hasseltine-Best
who rrork for the distrlct are consultants.
In our letter to Hr. Woska (supra) we stated that the
question was cLoser lrlth respect to lrlccill-Martin-Se lf . Pursuant
to the contract, representatives of lfcci ll-Martin-self rneet with
the distrlct to provide assumptlons and parameters for }and use
studies and future water service areas and facilities. 
Once the
parameters have been set, llccill-I{artin-Self wiII prepare three
land use naps based on the data, develop a plan for future treated
water services areas and work with the district to develop a list


Our File NO. I-9■-437
Page 8
of and potential sites for future facilities, corridors, and
canals ior servicing future needs and demands. ucci 11-l'tartin-Se If
will also neet with district staff to discuss service agreements
with local agencies and committees.
All of the above is perforured pursuant to a detail'ed contract
setting forth the expectations of the district with respect to the
naps. However, Ucci1l-Martin-Self also represents the district in
negotiations with the city of Antioch and contra costa concerning
the l,os vaqueros pipeline alignnent. The contract further
provides that there will be substantial interaction as the
Lontract enters later Phases. This cooperative working
relationship suggests nore than nere contract monitoring. Again,
rather than delivering a finished product to the district for the
district's use, lt appears that McGi1l-uartin-sel f is interacting
with the district rnuch as would district staff. 
Since you have
provided no facts contrary to this conclusion, we continue- to
telieve the enployees of McGi I l-Martin-self who srork for the
di.strlct are consultants under the Act.
5. fndependent conduct
Regulation 18700 (c) Provides:
(c) A publlc offlcial or designated ernployee
I'participates in the naking of a governuental
dLcisioni' when, acting wlthln the authority of his
or her posltlon, he or she:
(1) Negotiates, without siqnificant
substantive review, with a governmental entity
or private person regarding the decisioni or
(21 Advises or nakes recornrnendatlons to
the decision-naker, either directly or without
siqnificant intervenino substantive review,
by:
(A) Conducting research or rnaking
any investigation which reoulres the
exercise of iudcrment on the part of the
official or designated enployee and the
purpose of r.rhich is to influence the
decision; or
(B) Preparing or presenting any
report, analysis or opinion, orally or in
writing, whlch requlres the exercise of
judgroent on the part of the official or
designated enployee and the purpose of
which is to influence the decision.
(hphasis added. )

You have asked lrhether the definition of "participates in the
rnaking of a governmental decisionit is in conflict $,ith the
""""p€i"" in-Regnrlation 18700(a) (2). Regulation 18700(a) -(2) is
defi;itional. 
Houever, a person who qualifies as a consultant
un-er negulation 18700(a) (r) nay still not "partj'cipate in the

Sinilarly, in the Bge Advlce Letter, Nos. A-84-305 and

The lndependent, substantive and intervening

Holrever, the concept of tilndependent substantlve reviewrr has

by tire governmental- lgency, if the review by the agency was in

Flnal1y, please be advised that since the tlue you requested
advlce on Septlrnber 23, 199L, the Legislature passed.urgency

(a) A reglstered professlonal engineer or licensed
land surveyor l.Iho renders professional services as a

dlrectly or through a flrn ln which he or she is

rendeis'profeslional engineering or land surveylng
services- independently of the control and direction of

(c) Subdivision (a) does not apply to that portion
of the work that constitutes the recomnendation of the

district's

hundred fifty dollars (S250) or nore for

1. Natural Persons
A itconsultantrr is defined in Regulation 18700(a) (2) as
foI lows :
trConsultantrr shall include any natural person
who provides under contract, inforrnation, advice,
reconmendation or counsel to a state or l,ocal
government agency,
Regulation 18700 (a) (2).
According to Regiulation 18700(a) (2), a rrconsultantrr nust be a
natural person and cannot be a business entity.


Our Fi■e No. I-9■-437
The rationale behind liuriting the tern consultant to natural
persons is that the actual decisionnaker, as in the.case of a
lublic officia)., wilL be an individual. Moreover disclosure of
the interests of the fir!0 itself does not appear to reflect the
policy goals of the Act. The Act is intended to ensure that a
iuUlil 5fti"i.t 
wiII not be biased in performing his or her dutj.es
Ly the official's financial interests. (Section 81001(b).) The
disclosure of the interests of the firm, in the place of the
interests of the decisionnaker, in Dany cases will' not inforn the
public of such potential sources of bias.
For example, an emPloyee of a firn uray perforrn consulting
services for I public agency concerning a project that may affect
a client of the firrn. is an eurployee, the involvement of the
client nay have no significance to the. enployee-. On-the other
f'ri"a, it fhe ernptoyee ouns property which rrill be affected by his
oi 116. consultiirg iecisions, the enployee nay be biased in the
recornmendatlons he nakes to the publlc entity.
2. Decisionnakinq Authoritv
You have also asked if a person can be a consultant lf the
person does not trDake[ a final decision. Regutation 18700(a) (2)
provides that a ttconsultantrr is a person who provides under
iontract, information. advice, recornmendatio! 9r cqunsel to a
state or I@. 
ttre definition does not require
ifr.t tfr" person rrnake,t a flnal declsion. The conflict of interest
potentlal- ln a consulting arrangenent arises if the person may
ir.31srr or rinfluencerr governnental decisions to further the
person's own financial interests.
Thus, for exanple, where an offj.cial's recornrnendation to a
decisionuraker rnay bL biased, that person has a conflict of
interest. 
(Reguiation 18700.1.) ln addition, where the official
nakes the adcilion and has a financial stake in the decision, the
official has a conflict of interest. (Regulation 1870o.)
Regrulatlon 187oo does provide an exception to the definitlon
of 'rconiultantrr which reqJuires that the consultant conduct
research and arrlve at conclusions lrlth respect to his or her
renditlon of inforrnation, advice, reconnendation, or counsel
independent of control and direction of the agency, and that the
consultant possess no authority with respect to any agency
decision beyond the rendition of infonoation, advice,
reconmendation or counsel.

Hasseltine-Best would be different if the words rrunder the
